what: BSN=64 for non-split-K M<=32 K<=1024 fused shapes (from BSN=128) — doubles grid from ~90 to ~180 blocks for better CU utilization on small shapes. BSM=8 stays at 8. Currently BSN=128 NW=4 leaves shapes like 32x2880x512 with only 90 blocks (0.35 waves).
why: BSN=64 with NW=4 was tested for M=64 (v101, +73%) but NEVER tested for M<=32 K<=1024 shapes. These shapes have much less compute per block (K=512 means only 4 K-iters), so the tile efficiency vs CU utilization tradeoff may be different. With BSN=64: grid doubles, each block does half the N work but memory latency is better hidden by more concurrent blocks.
override: v161 and v162 both targeted M=64 (not M<=32 K<=1024). M<=32 shapes are memory-bound with K=512, fundamentally different from M=64 K=2048. BSN=64 NW=4 never tested for this specific path.

what_failed: Session 17: 1 approach tested after reviewing 228+ prior attempts and identifying exact LB shapes (4x2880x512, 16x2112x7168, 32x4096x512, 32x2880x512, 64x7168x2048, 256x3072x1536). BSN=64 for M<=32 K<=1024 regressed +1.0% (9.10 vs 9.01) -- smaller N tiles reduce per-block efficiency more than doubled CU utilization helps. All viable tile configs, split-K, cache modifiers, ASM kernels, kernel paths, dispatch optimizations, env vars exhausted across 229 attempts. The 9.5% gap to #1 (8.23us) likely requires automated hyperparameter search or a novel kernel architecture.
